answer:
**The Falsafa corpus does not contain explicit treatises, prefaces, or commentaries defining the ghazal form theoretically from the Urdu poets themselves.** Instead, it preserves the form through practice in the poetry collections *Diwan-e-Ghalib* (239 ghazals by Mirza Ghalib), *Diwan-e-Zauq* (60 ghazals by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq), and Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* parts (which include ghazals and other forms, with references to "ghazal" recitation).[^1][^2][^3]

As practiced here, the **ghazal** is a lyric poem in **verse layout**, structured as a sequence of 5–20 **shers** (couplets), with each sher marked as a distinct paragraph `[p-xxxxxx]`. Each sher comprises two **misras** (hemistichs or lines) that form a semantically autonomous unit, often shifting themes like unrequited love, longing (*hijr*), union (*visal*), rival (*hasham*), pain (*dard*), beauty, philosophy, or mysticism—typically addressing a beloved (*mahbub*) who may be human, metaphorical, or divine.[^4][^5][^6]

### Key Conventions Observed:
1. **Rhyme Scheme (Qafiya and Radif)**: All shers share an identical **radif** (refrain, a fixed word or phrase at each misra's end) and **qafiya** (rhyme immediately preceding it). The scheme is AA BA CA DA ..., creating unity.[^4][^5]
   - Example: In Ghalib's ghazal no. 6, every sher ends "... , Beloved" (radif: ", Beloved"; qafiya varies, e.g., "snuffed," "grace").[^7]
   - In Zauq's ghazal no. 26, every sher ends "left behind" (radif; qafiya: "slain," "rain").[^8]

2. **Matla (Opening Sher)**: The first sher rhymes fully in *both* misras (AA), introducing the radif/qafiya. Its title is often the chapter title (first line).[^4][^9]

3. **Maqta (Closing Sher)**: The final sher incorporates the poet's **takhallus** (pen name), e.g., "Ghalib" or "Zauq," for signature.[^5][^10][^11]

4. **Thematic and Stylistic Traits**:
   - **Monorhyme refrain** binds disparate images (e.g., wine, arrows, locks of hair, ruins).
   - **Ishq** (passionate love) dominates, with paradox (*tazad*), wit (*zubān-bāzī*), and Sufi undertones.
   - Couplets are punchy, epigrammatic; order may not be strictly narrative.
   - Mentions of "ghazal" itself (e.g., "ghazal-khwani" = reciting ghazals; "Ghalib has not sung a single ghazal today") confirm self-referential awareness.[^12][^13]

Iqbal adapts it philosophically (e.g., spiritual revival), while Ghalib and Zauq emphasize classical *ughniyyah* (lyric) refinement.[^14] For full immersion, read chapters sequentially—they embody the form.

[^1]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, metadata description ("complete collection of ghazals").
[^2]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, metadata ("complete collection of ghazals").
[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 27 (*Visal*), mentioning "meri ghazal khwani" ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-f82112](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/#p-f82112)).
[^4]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 6 (*Aamad-e-khat se hua hai sard jo bazaar-e-dost*), full ghazal ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/)).
[^5]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 26 (*Dard minnat-kash-e-dawa na hua*), full ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/)).
[^6]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 26 (*Kal gaye the tum jise bimaar-e-hijraan chhod kar*), full ([/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/26-kal-gaye-the-tum-jise-bimaar-e-hijraan-chhod-kar/translation/](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/26-kal-gaye-the-tum-jise-bimaar-e-hijraan-chhod-kar/translation/)).
[^7]: Ibid., ch. 6, e.g., para. [p-2705d0] ("I love this ghazal, Ghalib...") ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/#p-2705d0](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/#p-2705d0)).
[^8]: *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 26, e.g., [p-20030a] ("Recite a fiery ghazal, Zauq...") ([/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/26-kal-gaye-the-tum-jise-bimaar-e-hijraan-chhod-kar/translation/#p-20030a](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/26-kal-gaye-the-tum-jise-bimaar-e-hijraan-chhod-kar/translation/#p-20030a)).
[^9]: E.g., Ghalib ch. 6 matla: [p-da40bd] ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/#p-da40bd](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/translation/#p-da40bd)).
[^10]: Ghalib ch. 26 maqta: [p-57b20e] ([/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-57b20e](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-57b20e)).
[^11]: Zauq ch. 26 maqta: [p-20030a], supra.
[^12]: Ghalib ch. 6 [p-2705d0], supra.
[^13]: Ghalib ch. 26 [p-57b20e], supra.
[^14]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4 (mentioning Ghazali in a ghazal-like lament) ([/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-664e27](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-664e27)).
